01_go_main.title: Package Main and Entry Point
01_go_main.description:
An executable Go program uses `package main` and a `main()` function.

```go
package main

func main() {
    fmt.Println("hi")
}
```

q2.question: Why must an executable use `package main`?
options: The Go tool looks for a `main` package with a `main` function as the program entry.
Only `package main` can import the standard library.
It forces every variable to be global.
explanations: Yes. That is how the toolchain identifies an executable program.
Any package can import the standard library.
Package choice does not make variables global.
q2.answerReason: A runnable binary needs one entry package and one entry function the toolchain can invoke.

q3.question: What happens if a package is not named `main`?
options: It cannot contain any functions.
It automatically becomes a test-only package.
It can still be compiled as a library package, but not as the executable entry package.
explanations: Non-main packages absolutely can contain functions.
Package name alone does not make it test-only.
Correct. `main` is special because it marks the binary entry package.
q3.answerReason: Go distinguishes reusable packages from the program entry package through `package main`.

02_slices.title: Slice Views over Arrays
02_slices.description:
A slice is a small descriptor over an underlying array. It tracks pointer, length, and capacity.

```go
nums := []int{1, 2, 3}
part := nums[:2]
```

Mutating the shared region through one slice is visible through another.

q2.question: Why does changing `part[0]` affect `nums[0]`?
options: The slice still points to the same backing array.
Go automatically synchronizes equal indexes across all slices.
Slicing copies the whole array and merges it back later.
explanations: Correct. The view shares storage with the original array segment.
There is no special sync mechanism like that.
Basic slicing does not copy the entire array.
q2.answerReason: A slice is metadata around shared storage, not an independent container by default.

q3.question: What makes a slice different from an array in Go?
options: A slice stores elements inline and never shares backing storage.
A slice is a descriptor over an underlying array region.
A slice can only hold strings.
explanations: That describes arrays more than slices.
Correct. A slice carries pointer/length/capacity metadata over shared storage.
Slices can hold many element types.
q3.answerReason: Thinking of a slice as a view helps explain aliasing, append behavior, and shared mutation.

03_maps.title: Map Lookup Semantics
03_maps.description:
Map lookups can return a zero value and a boolean presence flag.

```go
v, ok := m["key"]
```

That second result is the safe way to distinguish missing keys from stored zero values.

q2.question: Why is the `ok` result useful?
options: It separates a missing key from a present key whose value happens to be the zero value.
It makes the lookup faster by skipping hashing.
It converts the map into an ordered structure.
explanations: Yes. Presence and value are distinct facts.
Hashing still happens.
Maps remain unordered.
q2.answerReason: Without `ok`, you cannot tell whether `0` means absent or explicitly stored.

q3.question: Why can `value` alone be ambiguous after a map lookup?
options: Because the zero value may mean either “missing key” or “present key with zero value”.
Because Go randomizes numeric results on missing keys.
Because map lookups always return strings internally.
explanations: Correct. That is why the boolean presence result exists.
Go does not randomize the zero value.
Map result types stay whatever the map value type is.
q3.answerReason: The extra boolean exists because absence and zero are distinct states.

04_struct_methods.title: Methods on Struct Receivers

q2.question: Why is the receiver a pointer here?
options: The method needs to mutate the original struct value.
Go requires all method receivers to be pointers.
Only pointer receivers can call `fmt.Println`.
explanations: Correct. Mutation must affect the same struct instance.
Value receivers also exist.
Printing has nothing to do with receiver kind.
q2.answerReason: If `Inc` used a value receiver, it would increment a copy instead of the caller-owned value.

q3.question: What would likely happen if `Inc` used a value receiver instead?
options: The program would stop compiling because methods need pointer receivers.
The call would mutate both the original and a hidden backup copy.
The method would increment a copy, leaving the caller-owned struct unchanged.
explanations: Go allows value receivers too.
There is no hidden backup synchronization here.
Correct. Value receivers work on a copy of the receiver.
q3.answerReason: Receiver choice is really about whether the method should observe/mutate the same value or a copy.

05_interfaces.title: Interfaces Describe Behavior
05_interfaces.description:
A Go interface is satisfied implicitly by method sets.

```go
type Runner interface {
    Run() string
}
```

There is no `implements` keyword; the type just needs the required methods.

q2.question: How does `Job` satisfy `Runner`?
options: It has the required `Run() string` method, so it satisfies the interface implicitly.
It must declare `implements Runner` in the type definition.
Interfaces are satisfied only at runtime through reflection.
explanations: Yes. Go uses structural typing for interfaces.
Go has no `implements` keyword.
The type checker verifies interface satisfaction statically.
q2.answerReason: Implicit satisfaction keeps interfaces lightweight and lets consumers define the behavior they need.

q3.question: Why is implicit interface satisfaction useful in Go?
options: It forces all packages to depend on one shared base interface package.
Consumers can define small interfaces based on needed behavior without modifying the concrete type.
It prevents compile-time checking of method sets.
explanations: It actually reduces that kind of coupling.
Correct. Small consumer-owned interfaces are one of Go’s strongest design patterns.
Interface satisfaction is still checked statically.
q3.answerReason: A package can accept behavior it needs without requiring the concrete type author to anticipate every interface.

06_goroutines.title: Goroutines and Concurrent Work
06_goroutines.description:
A goroutine is a lightweight concurrent function execution.

```go
go work()
```

Starting one does not block the caller. Coordination still needs channels, sync primitives, or context.

q1.question: What does this program print with the wait group in place?

q2.question: Why is `WaitGroup` used here?
options: To keep `main` alive until the goroutine finishes.
To make the goroutine run on a specific CPU core.
To share local variables by reference automatically.
explanations: Correct. Without synchronization, `main` could exit too early.
Core placement is not what `WaitGroup` does.
Variable capture is a different language behavior.
q2.answerReason: Concurrency starts work; synchronization decides when that work must finish before exiting or continuing.

q3.question: What would be the main risk if `main` returned immediately after starting the goroutine?
options: The process could exit before the goroutine prints anything.
The goroutine would automatically become a daemon saved for later.
The Go runtime would convert the goroutine into a channel.
explanations: Correct. Program lifetime still matters even with lightweight concurrency.
Goroutines do not survive process exit.
There is no such conversion.
q3.answerReason: Concurrency starts work, but you still need coordination when the whole process might end first.

07_channels.title: Channels Coordinate Ownership
07_channels.description:
Channels are typed conduits for communication between goroutines.

```go
ch := make(chan int)
ch <- 1
value := <-ch
```

They help structure concurrent ownership transfer instead of sharing mutable state freely.

q2.question: What is the main role of a channel?
options: It coordinates communication and handoff between goroutines.
It sorts messages before they are received.
It guarantees all goroutines run sequentially.
explanations: Yes. Communication is the core model.
Channels do not sort values.
Channels can synchronize, but they do not make the whole program sequential.
q2.answerReason: Go encourages structuring concurrency around communication, with channels making handoff explicit.

q3.question: Why do channels often reduce the need for shared mutable state?
options: Because they make all data global automatically.
Because receiving from a channel clones every object deeply.
Because values can be handed off between goroutines instead of being mutated concurrently in place.
explanations: Channels do not globalize data.
Channels do not imply deep cloning of everything sent.
Correct. The model encourages handoff and coordination rather than unrestricted shared mutation.
q3.answerReason: The slogan is not magic; it is about structuring ownership and communication explicitly.

08_context_timeout.title: Context Cancellation and Timeouts
08_context_timeout.description:
A `context.Context` carries cancellation, deadlines, and request-scoped values across call boundaries.

```go
ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(parent, time.Second)
defer cancel()
```

q2.question: Why pass `context.Context` through a request path?
options: It lets downstream work observe cancellation and deadlines consistently.
It replaces function parameters for normal business data.
It forces synchronous execution.
explanations: Correct. It propagates lifecycle signals.
Regular data should still use normal parameters or structs.
Context does not force synchronous behavior.
q2.answerReason: Cancellation only helps if every layer can see the same signal and stop work cooperatively.

q3.question: What kind of data should usually not be stuffed into `context.Context`?
options: Cancellation and deadline signals.
Normal business parameters that should be explicit function arguments.
Request-scoped values that middleware and handlers must share carefully.
explanations: Those are core context responsibilities.
Correct. Ordinary domain inputs are clearer as explicit parameters.
Some request-scoped metadata can live in context when used deliberately.
q3.answerReason: Context is for cross-cutting request lifecycle concerns, not for hiding normal function inputs.
